coredata.dc:title: Insight into the existent state of nitrogen-doped carbon dots in titanate nanotubes and their roles played toward simultaneous removal of coexisted Cu2+ and norfloxacin in water
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Kang, Li
Yang, Hanpei
Yu, Haibo
Wu, Qiangshun
coredata.dc:description: In this work, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) were introduced in different existent sites of titanate nanotubes (TNTs) by a facile synthesis, and their effects on surface potential, photoelectrochemical properties and simultaneous removal of coexisted Cu2+ and norfloxacin (NOR) performance in water were systematically investigated. Constructed NCDs-TNTs composite displayed superior performance towards the adsorption (ion exchange/coordination) of Cu2+ and adsorption-oxidization of NOR over the two individuals, mainly benefiting from the collaboration of NCDs in different existent states. The existence of TiNH chemical linkage was identified between TNTs and NCDs-OT (NCDs on the outer surface of TNTs), which not only modulates the surface potential to favor the external diffusion of Cu2+ or NOR+ from aqueous solution to the negatively charged NCDs-TNTs, but also facilitates the intraparticle transfer of contaminants to the reactive sites. In addition, the up-conversion light property of NCDs-OT and the interstitial NCDs-IT (NCDs on the inner surface of TNTs) doping in TNTs interact together to enable NCDs-IT-TNTs to fully absorb and utilize all visible light. The photoexcited electrons were further trapped by NCDs-OT to promote the photogenerated carrier separation. Adsorbed Cu2+ could also improve the performance of NCDs-TNTs toward NOR oxidization, mainly owing to the self-synchronous doping of adsorbed Cu2+ broadening light absorption area and acting as mediators for delivering electrons. This work provides unique insights into the structural design of composite materials for such combined contamination remediation in water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Titanate nanotubes
Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Existent state
Combined contamination

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Batch experiments of the coexisted Cu2+ and NOR removal
Successful introduction of NCDs in different existent sites of TNTs
Contribution of NCD with different existent states in TNTs to the improved performance
Collaboration between NCDs-IT and NCDs-OT for adsorption performance enhancement
Synergies between NCDs-IT and NCDs-OT for photocatalytic performance enhancement
Simultaneous removal of coexisted Cu2+ and NOR from water by NCD-TNTs
Performance comparison of simultaneous and individual removal of Cu2+ and NOR
Mechanism toward the enhanced photocatalytic performance by adsorbed Cu2+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns (a) and FT-IR spectra (b) of the as-prepared samples.
XPS survey spectra of the as-prepared samples (a); high-resolution XPS spectra of O 1 s (b), C 1 s (c), and N 1 s (d).
TEM (a-c) and high-resolution TEM (d-f) images of the as-prepared samples; HAADF-STEM elemental mapping images of NCDs-TNTs (g-l).
Adsorption isotherms of Cu2+ onto the as-prepared samples (a); adsorption kinetics from the data in (a) of Cu2+ initiated at 60 mg/L (b).
Zeta potentials (a); N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms (b) and BJH pore-size distributions (c) of the samples; XRD patterns of fresh and used NCDs-TNTs (d).
Removal of NOR on the three samples (a) and degradation reaction kinetics (b); adsorption of NOR on NCDs-TNTs and MNCDs-TNTs (c) and linear fitting to data in adsorption from b (d).

UV–vis absorption spectra (a) and Tauc plots (b) on the samples; up-conversion photoluminescence spectroscopy of NCDs (c); PL spectra (d), transient photocurrent responses (e) and EIS measurements (f) on samples.
Removal of NOR (a) and Cu2+ (c) on NCDs-TNTs in two different conditions and degradation reaction kinetics (b).

High-resolution XPS spectrum of Cu 2p3/2 on used NCDs-TNTs (a); UV–vis absorption spectra (b), PL spectra (c) and EPR spectra of DMPO-•OH (d) and DMPO-•O2 – (e) on the fresh and used NCDs-TNTs; Mott-Schottky plot on used NCDs-TNTs (f).
Schematic diagram for the coexisted Cu2+ and NOR removal on NCDs-TNTs.
3D EEMs of the NOR solution after 0 (a), 15 (b), 30 (c), 45 (d), 60 (e) and 120 min (f) under visible light irradiation.
Mass spectra of NOR solution at 0 (a), 15 (b), 45 (c) and 120 min (d).

head.ce:title: Insight into the existent state of nitrogen-doped carbon dots in titanate nanotubes and their roles played toward simultaneous removal of coexisted Cu2+ and norfloxacin in water

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Big Data and Visual Computing, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Yancheng Institute of Technology, Yancheng 224051, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Yancheng Institute of Technology, Yancheng 224051, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) were introduced in different existent sites of titanate nanotubes (TNTs) by a facile synthesis, and their effects on surface potential, photoelectrochemical properties and simultaneous removal of coexisted Cu2+ and norfloxacin (NOR) performance in water were systematically investigated. Constructed NCDs-TNTs composite displayed superior performance towards the adsorption (ion exchange/coordination) of Cu2+ and adsorption-oxidization of NOR over the two individuals, mainly benefiting from the collaboration of NCDs in different existent states. The existence of TiNH chemical linkage was identified between TNTs and NCDs-OT (NCDs on the outer surface of TNTs), which not only modulates the surface potential to favor the external diffusion of Cu2+ or NOR+ from aqueous solution to the negatively charged NCDs-TNTs, but also facilitates the intraparticle transfer of contaminants to the reactive sites. In addition, the up-conversion light property of NCDs-OT and the interstitial NCDs-IT (NCDs on the inner surface of TNTs) doping in TNTs interact together to enable NCDs-IT-TNTs to fully absorb and utilize all visible light. The photoexcited electrons were further trapped by NCDs-OT to promote the photogenerated carrier separation. Adsorbed Cu2+ could also improve the performance of NCDs-TNTs toward NOR oxidization, mainly owing to the self-synchronous doping of adsorbed Cu2+ broadening light absorption area and acting as mediators for delivering electrons. This work provides unique insights into the structural design of composite materials for such combined contamination remediation in water.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the rapid development of large-scale and intensive livestock breeding has generated a large amount of wastewater, which is often discharged directly into natural water bodies without special treatment, becoming one of the main sources of water pollution [1,2]. Heavy metals, especially Cu, essential for the growth of livestock, and antibiotics, especially norfloxacin (NOR), used to treat bacterial infections in livestock, are two of the main pollutants found in livestock wastewater [1,3]. In consequence, the combined contamination zones can be formed, posing an unpredictable threat to the ecological environment and human health [4,5]. However, many conventional treatment methods including physical, chemical and biological methods, are not suitable for the in-situ treatment of such combined polluted wastewater due to the complexity of the operational processes and high water quality standards [4,6,7]. Thus, the development of effective, inexpensive and environmentally friendly technologies to remediate such wastewater is urgently needed.

In the previous studies [8,9], iron monosulfide and graphite carbon nitride were integrated with titanate nanotubes to efficiently remove heavy metals (Cr) and antibiotics (sulfamethazine) in water, respectively. The unique advantages of titanate nanotubes have been discovered as both adsorbents for the removal of heavy metals via ion exchanges and photocatalysts for the degradation of antibiotics in water [10,11]. The appearance of titanate nanotubes is hollow with spiral faceted openings at both ends, and its basic configuration is parallel zigzag ribbons formed by triple [TiO6] octahedra with co-vertexes, which has interstitial H+/Na+ between the ribbons [9]. However, the activity of titanate nanotubes as photocatalysts is limited mainly by the fact that titanate nanotubes can only absorb and utilize ultraviolet light, which accounts for less than 5 % of solar light [7,12]. Furthermore, titanate nanotubes have a certain number of active sites when adsorbing heavy metals [11,13]. It can therefore be expected that a suitable semiconductor could be integrated with titanate nanotubes to simultaneously improve the removal efficiencies of coexisted Cu2+ and NOR in water.

Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs), as mono-dispersed spherical carbon nanomaterials less than 10 nm in size composed of a carbon core with sp2/sp3 hybridization and an outer layer with oxygen/nitrogen functional groups, have attracted widespread attention in the fields of photocatalysis and biomarker discovery due to their unique optical, electron transfer and biocompatible properties, low toxicity, wide range of sources and ease of synthesis [14,15]. Wang et al. [16] synthesized visible-light-driven NCDs/graphitic carbon nitride photocatalyst via a facile polymerized method for the efficient degradation of indomethacin. The water-soluble NCDs prepared by Wu et al. via a simple hydrothermal strategy was used for fluorescence detection of Cu2+, owing to the complexation between Cu2+ and the functional groups on the surface of NCDs resulting in the effective fluorescence quenching of NCDs [17]. Thus, NCDs is a very promising candidate to be considered in combination with TNTs for the efficient remediation of livestock wastewater. However, the influences of the existent state of NCDs in the intercalated materials on the properties are easily ignored but worth investigation. Furthermore, no attention has ever been paid to the roles of the different existent states of NCDs in intercalated materials, such as TNTs for simultaneous removal of coexisted Cu2+ and norfloxacin in water.

Based on the above analysis, NCDs were introduced in different existent sites of TNTs to fabricate NCDs-TNTs composite through a facile method for simultaneous removal of coexisted Cu2+ and NOR commonly occurring in livestock wastewater [18]. The main objectives of this study include: (1) Confirmation of the successful introduction of NCDs in different existent sites of TNTs; (2) Analysis of the effects of NCDs in different existent states on surface potential, photoelectrochemical properties and simultaneous removal of coexisted Cu2+ and NOR performance in water; (3) Investigation of the roles played by adsorbed Cu2+ in enhancing the photocatalytic activity of NCDs-TNTs; (4) Elaboration of the mechanism toward the removal of Cu2+ and NOR; (5) Elucidation of the possible degradation routes of NOR.

All chemicals in this study were analytical grade and used without further purification. TiO2 (P25, 21 nm) and NOR were obtained from Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ethyl alcohol, NaOH, citric acid monohydrate and ureophil were purchased from Nanjing Chemical Reagent Co., ltd. (Nanjing, China). Cu(NO3)2·6H2O (Kaitong Chemical Co., ltd., Tianjin, China) was used to prepare Cu2+ stock solutions (1000 mg/L) by diluting with deionized water. Deionized water was produced by UPT-11–10 T ultrapure water equipment (ULUPURE, China).

TNTs was prepared via one-step water hydrothermal method [7]. 1.2 g of TiO2 was dispersed into 8 mol/L NaOH (80 mL) with continuous magnetic stirring for 12 h. The above mixture was then poured into a 100 mL autoclave and reacted hydrothermally at 130 °C for 48 h. After the previous steps have been completed and the autoclave has cooled to room temperature, the obtained solution was centrifuged and washed 6 times with water and ethyl alcohol until the pH value of the solution reached neutral. The product was dried at 80 °C for 5 h to obtain TNTs.

NCDs was synthesized by a facial process according to the previous literature [19]. Firstly, 3 g of citric acid monohydrate was completely dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water. 3 g of ureophil was then added to the above solution by magnetically stirring for 30 min. The solution obtained was placed into a 25 mL autoclave with heating at 180 °C for 4.5 h. Afterwards, the atrovirens liquid was transferred into a semi-permeable membrane for 24 h to gain NCDs.

0.1 g of TNTs white powder was ultrasonically dissolved into 20 mL of ethyl alcohol for 20 min. Afterwards, 0.1, 0.3, 0.5 and 0.7 mL of NCDs were added into the above solution with ultrasonic dispersion for 30 min, respectively. The above mixture was transferred into a 25 mL autoclave with heating at 120 °C for 4 h. Lastly, the products were washed 2 times with ethyl alcohol and then dried at 60 °C for 10 h to obtain 1-NCDs-TNTs (0.1 mL NCDs/0.1 g TNTs), 3-NCDs-TNTs (0.3 mL NCDs/0.1 g TNTs), 5-NCDs-TNTs (0.5 mL NCDs/0.1 g TNTs) and 7-NCDs-TNTs (0.7 mL NCDs/0.1 g TNTs), respectively. Detailed equipment descriptions and measurement methods were included in the supplementary materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Batch experiments of the coexisted Cu2+ and NOR removal
ce:section.ce:para: Batch experiments toward the isolated Cu2+ removal were conducted at room temperature. The adsorption isotherm tests were performed by adjusting the initial Cu2+ concentration from 10 to 200 mg/L (100 mL), and then adding 0.5 g/L of each as-prepared material to the obtained solution and adjusting the initial pH to 5.0 (±0.1) by 0.01 mol/L HCl. The mixture was kept stirring for 180 min to reach adsorption equilibrium to collect samples for determination. The adsorption kinetic tests were carried out under similar operations. Each as-prepared material (0.5 g/L) was placed into 60 mg/L of Cu2+ solution with initial pH at 5.0 (±0.1). Samples were obtained at various time intervals (0–180 min), and Cu2+ concentration was determined on an inductively coupled plasma-optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES, PerkinElmer Optima 7300 V, USA).

Batch experiments toward the isolated NOR removal were carried out in a 250 mL photoreactor. 50 mg of each as-prepared material was dissolved into 100 mL of NOR solution (5 mg/L) and the initial pH of the mixed solution was adjusted to 5.0 (±0.1) using 0.01 mol/L HCl. The mixture was stirred in the dark for 60 min to ensure adsorption–desorption equilibrium before the light illumination. A 300 W xenon lamp (CEL-HXUV300, CEAULIGHT, Beijing, China) equipped with an ultraviolet cut-off filter (λ ≥ 420 nm) was employed as the light source. Then, the mixture was placed under light irradiation for 120 min. During the adsorption and photoreaction, the collected samples were measured using a UV–vis spectrophotometer at 276 nm [20]. The performances of 1-NCDs-TNTs, 3-NCDs-TNTs, 5-NCDs-TNTs and 7-NCDs-TNTs on Cu2+ and NOR removal were evaluated in Fig. S2, with 5-NCDs-TNTs performing best among all composites. In the subsequent discussions, 5-NCDs-TNTs was labeled as NCDs-TNTs.

Batch experiments toward the coexisted Cu2+ and NOR removal were then performed. Briefly, 100 mL of NOR (5 mg/L) and Cu2+ (60 mg/L) solution was completely mixed with each as-prepared material (0.5 g/L). The remaining steps follow the same procedure as the previous NOR removal experiments. The samples acquired were used to determine both Cu2+ and NOR concentrations. All experiments in this study were performed three times and the data were averaged over duplicate measurements.

After adsorption and photoreaction, the used material was separated from the solution under vacuum filtration using a 0.22 µm aqueous filter membrane and air-dried for reuse. Subsequently, the material was successively immersed in 0.2 mol/L HNO3 to desorb Cu2+ and 0.2 mol/L NaOH to restore the H+/Na+ ratio [9,21]. The regenerated material was treated by operations in section 2.2.2 and reused in another five rounds.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Successful introduction of NCDs in different existent sites of TNTs
ce:section.ce:para: The crystallographic configurations of the prepared samples revealed by their X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns in Fig. 1 a show the diffraction peaks located at 24.6°, 28.3° and 48.4° matching to the crystal planes (110), (211) and (020) in TNTs (JCPDS No. 72–0148), respectively [9]. In addition, a distinct diffraction peak ascribing to the interlayer slit of TNTs was noted at a 2 theta of ca. 10°, and an interlayer distance (d) of ca. 0.884 nm could be calculated from the Bragg equation (2dsinθ = λ) [22]. The XRD pattern of NCDs shows only one weaker peak at ca. 27° (d≈0.324 nm) belonging to (002) facet of its graphitic phase (JCPDS No. 87–1526), which also indicates its presence in a largely amorphous carbon phase [7,23]. The peaks exhibited in XRD pattern of NCDs-TNTs are similar to those of TNTs. In comparison with TNTs, the peak of interlayer distance in NCDs-TNTs has an obvious rightward shift with a corresponding reduction in its d value to ca. 0.833 nm, suggesting the insertion of partial NCDs into the interlayers in TNTs (NCDs on the inner surface of TNTs was labeled as NCDs-IT) [9,24].

As illustrated in Fig. 1b, the surface microstructures of samples were investigated by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy. Apparently, all samples have –OH bands of adsorbed H2O peaks at 3445 (stretching vibration) and 1643 cm−1 (bending vibration), and superficial –OH bands at 3120 (stretching vibration) and 1396 cm−1 (bending vibration) [25]. The absorption bands centered at 902 and 463 cm−1 in TNTs are assigned to Ti-O stretching and [TiO6] deformation, respectively [24]. Spectrum with bands at 3304, 1729, 1319 and 1169 cm−1 were recorded on NCD, corresponding to the vibrations of NH, CO, CN and -COC- bonds, respectively [16,26,27]. In contrast, the bands in NCDs-TNTs are a combination of those in TNTs and NCDs, and the partial disappearance of bands may be due to lower contents of NCDs. However, the NH band in NCDs-TNTs shifts to the lower wavenumber at 3263 cm−1, probably owing to the formation of TiNH chemical linkage between -N-H2 on NCDs-OT (NCDs on the outer surface of TNTs) and –OH on TNTs as [28]:
[NCDs]-NH(H) + HO–[TNTs] → [NCDs]-NH-[TNTs]

The surface elemental compositions of samples were further validated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis. As shown in Fig. 2 a, XPS survey spectrum recorded on NCDs-TNTs reveals the existence of Ti, O, Na, C and N elements, confirming the successful fabrication of the composite. The high-resolution O 1 s spectrum on TNTs in Fig. 2b shows two distinct peaks at about 532 and 530 eV, which are attributed to superficial O and internal lattice O, respectively [22]. However, the slight shift in lattice O peak occurs on NCDs-TNTs, which may be ascribed to the interstitial doping of NCDs-IT in TNTs [29]. As for C 1 s spectrum on NCDs-TNTs in Fig. 2c, there are three peaks at 288.6, 286.4 and 284.8 eV, belonging to CN/CO, CN/CO, and CC/CC, respectively [16,19,30]. In addition, the N 1 s may be split into two peaks at 401.5 and 399.2 eV (Fig. 2d), ascribing to the NH and CN bonds, respectively [31].

The morphology of sample examined by transmission electron microscopy and scanning electron microscope (TEM and SEM) in Fig. 3 a and Fig. S1a demonstrates the hollow and open-ended tubular structure of TNTs with a length-to-diameter ratio of approximately 10:1. NCDs presented in Fig. 3b and Fig. S1b are homogeneously distributed nanoparticles less than 10 nm in size, being uniformly loaded onto the outer surface of TNTs as shown in Fig. 3c and Fig. S1c. The lattice fringes observed in the high-resolution TEM images of TNTs and NCDs (Fig. 3d and e) are approximately 0.88 and 0.32 nm, respectively. As a comparison, the lattice fringes of TNTs in the composite have a distinct change with a reduction to 0.83 nm in Fig. 3f, probably owing to the insertion of NCDs-IT into the interstitial spaces inside TNTs [24], which is consistent with the results obtained by XRD patterns in Fig. 1a. In addition, HAADF-STEM elemental mapping images in Fig. 3g-l display the existence of Ti, O, Na, C and N in NCDs-TNTs composite and the good distribution of each element remaining available after the combination of NCDs and TNTs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Contribution of NCD with different existent states in TNTs to the improved performance
Collaboration between NCDs-IT and NCDs-OT for adsorption performance enhancement
ce:section.ce:para: Roles of NCDs played toward the improved adsorption performance were investigated by Cu2+ adsorption on the as-prepared materials and the results displayed in Fig. 4 a demonstrate the well-fitted Langmuir isotherms (R 2 > 0.990, Eq. S3 and S4 in the supplementary materials), revealing its chemisorption nature [9]. The simulated isotherm model parameters are presented in Table S1 and the maximum adsorption capacities of Cu2+ on NCDs, TNTs and NCDs-TNTs at equilibrium are estimated to be 61.16, 106.38 and 130.77 mg/g, respectively, indicating that the introduction of NCDs in TNTs increased the maximum adsorption capacity by ∼ 23 % compared to pristine TNTs. Pseudo-second-order linearity can well match the adsorption kinetic data in Fig. 4b (R 2 > 0.995), further evidencing the chemo-interaction between Cu2+ and NCDs-TNTs. The values of q e and k (q e and k are the kinetic model parameters in Eq. S5 and 6) for NCDs-TNTs are approximately 1.2 times of those for TNTs, suggesting the improved adsorption performance in both thermodynamics and kinetics.

As shown in Fig. 5b, N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms performed on pristine and composite TNTs feature typical type IV isotherms, demonstrating their mesoporous nature [34]. The specific surface area of composite TNTs (265.87 m2/g) is higher than that of the pristine one (245.32 m2/g). However, the values of average pore size (Ddes) and pore volume (Vdes) of NCDs-TNTs in Fig. 5c are approximately equal to those of TNTs, which may be associated with the different existent states of NCDs in TNTs. Fig. 5d illustrates that the interlayer distance of used NCDs-TNTs (0.792 nm) is significantly smaller than that of the fresh one (0.833 nm), indicating the preferential ion exchange of Cu2+ (62 pm) with Na+ (97 pm) rather than H+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synergies between NCDs-IT and NCDs-OT for photocatalytic performance enhancement
ce:section.ce:para: As illustrated in Fig. 6 a, NCDs-TNTs have relatively stronger dark adsorption to NOR compared to the individuals, probably relating to their different zeta potentials. Upon the introduction of light irradiation, TNTs have no degradation performance towards NOR, mainly owing to their inability to be excited by visible light. However, the introduction of NCDs in TNTs achieved a significant improvement in NOR photodegradation performance, with a much higher removal rate on NCDs-TNTs (99.2 %) than on NCDs (23.8 %) within 120 min. The photodegradation process of NOR matched the pseudo-first-order reaction kinetics in Fig. 6b and the highest rate constant was achieved on NCD-TNTs (0.0342 min−1), which was about 18 times of that on NCDs (0.0019 min−1). Moreover, the influence of NCDs-TNTs dosage on NOR removal was further investigated. Fig. S3 reveals that as the dosage of NCDs-TNTs increased, the degradation rate constant of NOR first increased significantly to a maximum value of 0.0342 min−1 and then decreased slightly. This is mainly due to the fact that an increase in the dosage of NCDs-TNTs can provide more active sites for NOR degradation, while an increase in the turbidity of solution over a certain dosage can diminish the light absorption.

Some possible factors are considered to explore the potential reasons for the enhanced photocatalytic performance of TNTs after the introduction of NCDs-IT and NCDs-OT. The role of TiNH chemical linkage between NCDs-OT and TNTs towards the internal mass transfer of NOR in NCDs-TNTs was confirmed by performing a linear fit to NOR adsorption data on NCDs-TNTs and MNCDs-TNTs (Mechanical mixing of NCDs and TNTs) in Fig. 6c, according to the well-known mass transfer model proposed by Weber and Morris (Eq. S7). Fig. 6d demonstrated a good linear relationship (R 2 > 0.990) between q t and t1/2 for both NCDs-TNTs and MNCDs-TNTs at two distinct stages (I: external diffusion; II: intraparticle diffusion). The fitting results also reveal significantly higher values of K 1d (0.507 mg·g−1·min−1/2) and K2d (0.074 mg·g−1·min−1/2) on NCD-TNTs than those on MNCD-TNTs (K 1d and K 2d are 0.081and 0.034 mg·g−1·min−1/2, respectively), implying that the formation of TiNH chemical linkage not only facilitates the diffusion of NOR from water to the material surface by modulating the surface potential but also favors the internal diffusion of NOR to release more active sites, further facilitating the subsequent photodegradation process.

The optical properties of samples were further analyzed by measuring their UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra. As shown in Fig. 7 a, a noticeable red-shift from 375 to 535 nm was observed in the absorption band of composite TNTs compared to the pristine due to the introduction of NCDs-OT with strong absorption intensities in both ultraviolet and visible light region (image inserted in Fig. 7a) or NCDs-IT interstitial doping of TNTs. Based on the well-known Kubelka-Munk equation [34], the data in Fig. 7a were Tauc plotted, and it can be seen in Fig. 7b that the introduction of NCDs-IT has significantly reduced the optical band gap energies (Eg) of TNTs from the original 3.15 to 2.16 eV, mainly resulting from the interstitial doping of TNTs by NCDs-IT.

Moreover, the up-converted photoluminescence (UCPL) spectroscopy was performed on NCDs-OT to explore its potential for broadening the visible absorption spectrum of TNTs. As shown in Fig. 7c, the long-wavelength light (500–750 nm) in the visible region used to excite NCDs-OT can radiate short-wavelength light concentrated in the range of about 300–500 nm, implying that NCDs-IT-TNTs can fully absorb and utilize the light in the entire visible region through the UCPL of the surface NCDs-OT. After absorbing light, photocatalysts can be excited to generate photogenerated electron-hole (e--h+) pairs, and their migration and separation efficiency will also influence the photocatalysis performance. Fig. 7d reveals a significant quenched fluorescence in the photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy of NCDs-TNTs compared to TNTs, suggesting an efficiently suppressed recombination between e- and h+ [25,35]. As anticipated, the photocurrent density recorded on NCDs-TNTs in Fig. 7e is approximately-six times higher than that on TNTs, revealing the faster charge migration rate. It is mainly attributed to the electrophilic nature of doping N in the conjugated C plane of NCDs-OT [16]. The lone-pair electrons in the doping N extend outside the ring to form a weak p-π bond, rather than being directly involved in the closed-loop conjugation. Due to the large electronegativity of N, the attraction to the electron cloud in the ring leads to a decrease in the density of the electron cloud, and then the electrophile is displayed as a whole. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) on the samples in Fig. 7f further supports the above results, with the smaller radius of Nyquist curve on NCDs-TNTs representing the lower charge transfer resistance [36,37].

In general, the synergistic effect of NCDs-IT and NCDs-OT in enhancing the photocatalytic performance of TNTs is owing to the following two aspects: (1) the unique UCPL property of NCDs-OT allows NCDs-IT-doped TNTs to fully absorb and utilize all visible light; (2) the enhanced visible light absorption induced by NCDs-IT can provide more photogenerated charge, which facilitates the trapping of photogenerated e- by NCDs-OT.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous removal of coexisted Cu2+ and NOR from water by NCD-TNTs
Performance comparison of simultaneous and individual removal of Cu2+ and NOR
ce:section.ce:para: As can be seen from Fig. 8 a, a slight decrease in the dark adsorption capacity of NOR on NCD-TNTs is observed in coexisted Cu2+ and NOR removal compared to that in isolated NOR removal, which may be attributed to the competitive absorption between NOR+ and Cu2+. However, a significant increase in the photocatalytic performance of NCDs-TNTs (Fig. 8b) occurs in Cu2+ and NOR co-existence with NOR photodegradation rate (0.0473 min−1) being 1.4 times higher than that in NOR alone (0.0342 min−1).

Furthermore, Fig. 8c demonstrates that the presence of NOR has a slight influence on the Cu2+ adsorption performance of NCDs-TNTs. The results of recycling experiments illustrated in Fig. S4 demonstrate that the performance of NCDs-TNTs on the coexisted Cu2+ and NOR removal is relatively stable after five cycles, suggesting its good reusability. In addition, the performance of NCDs-TNTs was superior to other recently reported nanomaterials for the removal of similar combined pollutants (Table S2), suggesting its great potential in environmental remediation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism toward the enhanced photocatalytic performance by adsorbed Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: To further investigate the mechanisms responsible for the enhanced photocatalytic performance of NCDs-TNTs, the high-resolution XPS spectrum of Cu 2p3/2 on used NCDs-TNTs (material collected after removing coexisted Cu2+ and NOR on NCDs-TNTs) in Fig. 9 a shows two distinguishable peaks at approximately 934.9 and 932.8 eV, belonging to Cu2+ and Cu monomer, respectively [18]. Cu monomer was derived from the trapping of photogenerated e- by adsorbed Cu2+. Fig. 9b depicts the optical response of used NCDs-TNTs red-shifted from 535 to 604 nm compared to the fresh, while the calculated Eg of used NCDs-TNTs is reduced from 2.16 to 1.78 eV, mainly due to the self-synchronous doping of adsorbed Cu2+ in the interlayers of TNTs during the reaction [38]. As indicated in Fig. 9c, an obvious decrease in PL emission can be observed on used NCDs-TNTs compared to the fresh, probably due to the recombination repression of photo-excited carriers induced by the doped Cu2+ trapping e-.

Two common reaction species (•OH and •O2 –) potentially associated with NOR photodegradation on catalysts were measured by electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) technique with 5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline (DMPO) as the scavenger. As can be seen from Fig. 9d and e, no characteristic signal of DMPO-•OH adduct (four peaks with an intensity ratio of 1:2:2:1) appears, while a sextuple peak signal of DMPO-•O2 – adduct can be detected with greater signal intensity on used NCDs-TNTs than that on the fresh, implying •O2 – as the main active species [7,39]. As •O2 – is mainly acquired through the trapping of photo-excited e- by dissolved O2 in aqueous, it may be related to a preference of the doped Cu2+ to act as a medium for delivering electrons rather than as a trapping vessel (Cu2+ can be converted to Cu+ by gaining e-, while instable Cu+ will be further transformed to Cu2+ by giving e-). The positive slope of Mott-Schottky curve recorded on used NCDs-TNTs in Fig. 9f illustrates the n-type semiconductor [40], while a flat-band potential (E fb) value of −0.35 V versus NHE was obtained. Moreover, the conduction band (CB) potential of n-type semiconductor can be derived from E fb-0.1, and the band structure of used NCDs-TNTs can be calculated by combining the Eg obtained in Fig. 9b [22]. In summary, Fig. 10 exhibits a schematic diagram for coexisted Cu2+ and NOR removal on NCD-TNTs.

As shown in Fig. 11 , three-dimensional excitation-emission matrix fluorescence spectroscopy (3D EEMs) was performed to index the mineralization degree of NOR at various times during the photodegradation process. A distinct fluorescence peak focused on excitation/emission regions at approximately 260–320/400–480 nm in Fig. 11a is derived from the raw NOR solution, which refers to a humic acid-like organic falling in the region V in five 3D EEM regions [41]. The intensity of fluorescence peak in Fig. 11b enhances at 15 min owing to the more humic acid-like materials generated during NOR degradation/mineralization. The density of central fluorescence decreases with increasing light irradiation time (Fig. 11c-e), implying a gradual reduction in NOR concentration with further mineralization. Importantly, no significant fluorescence peak in Fig. 11f could be detected at 120 min and about 89 % TOC removal was achieved in comparison to the initial TOC content, revealing a large proportion of NOR molecules to be degraded/mineralized.

To investigate the mechanism towards the NOR photodegradation, the intermediates in the degradation process were further identified via mass spectrograph (MS) in Fig. 12 . As presented in Fig. 12a, the peak with m/z = 320 corresponds to NOR parent molecule according to the previous literature [42,43], while the other spurious peaks are induced from other materials in solution or intrinsic to the instrument [20].

After the introduction of light irradiation for 15 min, a significant decrease in the intensity of peak belonging to NOR can be observed in Fig. 12b with the appearance of some new peaks, implying the degradation of NOR and generation of intermediate products, which is consistent with the results obtained from the analysis in Fig. 11b. With the increase of light irradiation, the intensities of peaks attributed to NOR and some intermediates with relatively high m/z = 350, 334, 322, 302, 279 and 274 have reduced with the occurrence of two new peaks (m/z = 183 and 146) in Fig. 12c, while the intensities of peaks related to some intermediates with lower m/z = 251, 233 and 230 have enhanced, demonstrating the continuous oxidation of NOR and intermediates. As can be seen in Fig. 12d, the peaks for NOR and the previously appeared intermediates are weaker in intensity with the accompanying addition of two peaks with lower m/z = 79 and 88, implying the oxidation of a large portion of NOR.

As a result, two possible photodegradation pathways were proposed in Fig. 13 through analyzing the above detected intermediates. In pathway I, the piperazinyl ring in NOR was firstly attacked by •O2 – to form I-NOR1 owing to the higher electron density [44]. Four intermediates (I-NOR2, I-NOR3, I-NOR4 and I-NOR5) were derived from ring-opening and partial elimination reactions occurred on the piperazinyl ring [42,45,46]. Subsequently, I-NOR5 was converted to I-NOR6 via defluorination [46,47], followed by deamidation and destruction of the benzene ring resulting in the formation of I-NOR7 and NOR8 [48]. As described in pathway II, NOR parent molecule was successively transformed into II-NOR1, II-NOR2 and II-NOR3 through the loss of -F, -C2H5 and –COOH [49,50]. Then, the elimination of piperazine ring induced the appearance of II-NOR4, which was further attacked by •O2 – to generate NOR8 and NOR9. Based on the findings in Fig. 11f and 12d, most of the intermediates may be oxidized/mineralized into small inorganic molecules such as CO2, H2O, NH4 +, NO3 – and F- after 120 min of visible light irradiation [20].

In this study, NCDs were successfully introduced in different existent sites of TNTs to achieve simultaneous and efficient removal of coexisted Cu2+ and NOR from water. The excellent performance of NCDs-TNTs toward Cu2+ and NOR removal was mainly attributed to the different roles played by NCDs in different existent states of TNTs. The collaboration between NCDs-IT and NCDs-OT improves the Cu2+ adsorption performance on TNTs. NCDs-OT firstly makes Cu2+ more accessible to the negatively charged TNTs by modulating the surface potential, then Cu2+ was removed by ion exchange with Na+ in TNTs and coordination with NCDs-IT and NCDs-OT. The synergistic effect was evidenced between NCDs-IT and NCDs-OT in enhancing the photocatalytic performance of TNTs, which was mainly due to the unique UCPL property of NCDs-OT enabling NCDs-IT-doped TNTs to fully absorb and utilize all visible light, and the enhanced visible light absorption induced by NCDs-IT can provide more photogenerated charge to facilitate NCDs-OT trapping more photoexcited e-. The self-synchronous doping of adsorbed Cu2+ not only broadens the photo-absorption band but also serves as a medium for trapping and transferring e- to inhibit e- and h+ recombination, thus further improving the photocatalytic performance of NCDs-TNTs. Two probable NOR photodegradation pathways have been deduced through the combination of 3D EEMs and MS. The composite material with a unique configuration offers an environmentally friendly and economically efficient alternative for treating such combined pollution wastewater.

Li Kang: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Hanpei Yang: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Haibo Yu: Writing – review & editing. Qiangshun Wu: Data curation, Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 52070070 and 62106237), the Joint Funds of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. U21A20524), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (Grant No. B200203101) and the Shanxi Province Science Foundation for Youths (Grant No. 201901D211237).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2022.08.028.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Li, D. Dong, L. Zhang, X. Hua, Z. Guo, Photodegradation of norfloxacin in ice: Role of the fluorine substituent, Chemosphere (2021) 133042.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Near-infrared responsive upconversion glass-ceramic@BiOBr heterojunction for enhanced photodegradation performances of norfloxacin

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Li, S. Huang, N. Zhu, H. Yuan, D. Ge, Near-infrared responsive upconversion glass-ceramic@BiOBr heterojunction for enhanced photodegradation performances of norfloxacin, J. Hazard. Mater. 403 (2021) 123981.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Impact of dissolved organic matter on the photolysis of the ionizable antibiotic norfloxacin

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Liang, H. Zhao, M. Deng, X. Quan, S. Chen, H. Wang, Impact of dissolved organic matter on the photolysis of the ionizable antibiotic norfloxacin, J. Environ. Sci. 27 (2015) 115-123.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhanced visible light photocatalytic performance with metal-doped Bi2WO6 for typical fluoroquinolones degradation: Efficiencies, pathways and mechanisms

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Zhu, Y. Lv, J. Li, J. Ding, X. Xia, L. Wei, J. Jiang, G. Zhang, Q. Zhao, Enhanced visible light photocatalytic performance with metal-doped Bi2WO6 for typical fluoroquinolones degradation: Efficiencies, pathways and mechanisms, Chemosphere 252 (2020) 126577.
